segments.text: And Franks, hopefully can promote him to a
panellist please, team,
from 8:00 till 8:10.
Just coming through now, Mr. Chair.
Thanks very much. Good morning, Stephen.
Got
it there.
Yeah, we can hear you there. We can't see you, but that's okay.
In the interest of time, we can kick in, and I know you know this
drill.
Okay.
Oh, there we go.
Thank you. The
bill is very welcome to us, and particularly its intentions,
and we endorse it. We've also had an opportunity to see
submission by Federated Farmers and Federated Mountain Clubs and
others on similar issues on the position on the implications of the
health and safety law for recreational users, and we
endorse theirs strongly.
To be helpful, I just say, looking at other submissions as well,
I'm conscious that lawyers
typically are very poor at looking at
trading off cost and benefit.
They work in a world of Rolls Royce justice, where time and
money
matters to clients, but not to them and not to the courts.
And the whole climate is of sort of performative
virtue,
and a view that it's wrong to take account of costs
when some right or some other interest is at stake.
I mention that because I think some of the submissions that you're
facing about
the risks of allowing more freedom of action
for
workers, obviously, but more importantly for us, recreational users,
are very easy to highlight.
But the costs of a change in our culture away
from a general assumption that
people can make their own decisions and take risks and
do dangerous things like climbing and mountain biking and riding
motorcycles and all these other things, they are inherently
inimical to the approach
of a law that was intended to protect workers.
And, of course, the assumption is that workers have some kind of
compulsion or coercion. They are afraid of losing
their jobs or of other adverse consequences if
they turn down risk. That is not the case.
The law does not need to think
of that for recreational users.
They take risks deliberately all the time, so that
the starting position should be a carve-out for
recreational use. And what we're concerned about is
that when the bill was last amended in this area, when the act
was last amended in this area,
and it was seen as a concession to farmers,
it wasn't done very well. My own personal experience, I've got a couple of
thousand hectares that I've let a lot of people use.
We had a farm stay operation there that helped
defray the expenses of our caretaker.
Twelve years ago, the caretaker's family, who
do work on a lot of neighboring farms, got briefings on health and
safety risk, and they stopped. They weren't willing to
continue to operate. They just could not be reassured
that they weren't facing crippling liabilities for
foolish acts by people coming onto the farm. And I hate that.
I'd love to be able to be generous with that land.
So we looked at the bill.
I think the primary thing is to make sure that Section
doesn't undermine the effect of the exceptions intended in
37. Section 36
wasn't changed and should have been when Section 37 had the
farmland or the farm hazard exception.
Another primary recommendation is that
for recreational users, there'd be a
defense of reasonableness.
Essentially, a defense that if this is a risk I would
willingly take ex ante, I should be allowed to take it,
and that it isn't the same situation as, for example,
forcing mandatory helmet wearing on a whole lot of people who probably
don't want to have to carry helmets.
But where they are employees,
for the sake of the integrity of the law,
they've made that mandatory. But for recreational users,
there are many activities which have both the
natural risks of the environment, but also risks that
could be exacerbated,
for example, by farm tracks. I use that as
an example in the submission because it was one of the things that
my
farm manager pointed to when I was trying to reassure him
that Parliament had
given them some protection. He said, "This doesn't say anything about tracks.
That's where work happens all the time.
Tracks wash out.
We have an open digging for a culvert. It might sit there for three months.
I don't want to be
fined $600,000 because I didn't put a sign by the culvert
or cover it up immediately." And we need to think about these for all of the
recreational activities.
So I don't want to use up all the time.
If people have got questions, please ask, and I hope that
our solicitorAlex Ali
Miller is also online. She's familiar with the law and would be happy
to jump in and answer any technical questions.
Great. Thank you, Stephen and Clark.
If Alex is online, please put her as a panelist.
Any questions for Stephen?
Stephen, one of the points you've touched on is this challenge with the
culture that we are
seeing of businesses managing to the
letter of the law that's been raised in a number of the submissions, and a
concern that changing the act will
encourage people even further to manage to the letter of the law, but not actually
achieve
appropriate health and safety outcomes.
Have you got a comment on how we can strike that balance without leaving
the lawyers in the middle of it too much?
I think the biggest change, always the law is what is
enforced and how it's enforced. And
if you had a defense
that allowed you to
ask the court to consider what a person
in the shoes of the parties at the relevant
time would choose to do, that
would go a long way to getting away from the technicality of
compliance.
And there's an awful lot of performative compliance checklists and
notices galore, and it's the
same thing as the cone culture,
the
road cone culture that's dominated New Zealand. It's all performative.
And if there was a defense that said,
if we considered but decided it was reasonable
for people to be expected to move past this
obstruction
without being guided by cones,
because there's only five vehicle movements a day
on that road,
and it doesn't need a stop-go man at each end of the excavation.
I'm talking here of personal experience.
We've got five-
Mm
... kilometers of public road, we're the only users.
And now when they have to mend it or clear a drain,
they have five people involved, whereas,
10 years ago there was one with a digger.
And that was rational because the likelihood of someone coming upon
that
machine a- and, being injured was very low.
It's quite rational to say you do not need the entire
cabal of
basically back protection. So I would say the biggest
change would be by creating a defense
that enabled the calculation of risk against
cost
and considering it in relation to other risks.
There are thousands of deliberate crimes,
we've got an enormous amount of
legal talent gets drawn into
telling people how to cover their backs against risks that are
truly remote and are a simple reasonableness decision
that took account of cost and benefits would go a long way.
Thanks very much, Stephen. I think that that's all we've got time for this morning.
Thanks, Ali, for joining as well,
and I'm sure you'll continue to track progress of the bill.
Thank you, Kyle.
Members, we are now moving to the submission by Young
Workers Resource Center,
and so if we can bring them up to the...
And Matariki is
presenting on behalf. We've got 10 minutes with you this morning, Matariki.
Over to you.
Kia ora tatou. Ko Matariki Roche toku ingoa.
He kaimahi au ki Te Whare Kai Mahi Rangatahi. I'm Matariki.
I'm the comms and engagement coordinator at the Young Workers Resource
Center.
For any of you who haven't heard about our mahi, we have
two kind of main streams of mahi. One is going out into
high schools and community groups and teaching young peoples about their
rights at work,
hopefully before they have a negative experience in the workplace.
And then the other arm of our mahi is our help center, where
we provide advice, guidance, representation,
advocacy to ununionized young workers who are needing support.
So through both arms of these mahi, we talk a lot about health and
safety, and we educate and support young people
in their health and safety concerns.
Yeah, so in our written submission, I won't
reiterate it too much, but essentially we thoroughly
oppose this bill.
We have deep concerns about how this is going to impact young workers,
and we foresee that if this bill passes, young workers will
be less safe in the workplace.
We are particularly concerned at the
reduction of responsibilities for employers with less than
20 workers. Young workers are
over-represented,
or more likely to be in these small businesses.
And we're particularly concerned about the implications on
trainings.
For young workers,
people don't just learn how to be,
are born rather, with the knowledge of how to be
healthy and safe at work. They need to be taught it.
And then on top of that, we're also particularly concerned
about how these changes essentially
remove obligations for employers to care or do
anything about psychosocial harm,
which is of the most concern to young workers.
Those psychosocial harms are the ones that are
constantly raised to us.So I
wanted to put a bit of a
story and some real people behind those words that we've said
in our written submissions, and I wanted to share with you today a story
of a young worker,
who for the purpose of this submission, I will call Chris.
They have been anonymized though.
This young worker came through to us within the last 12
months.
He worked in a business with less than 20 employees,
aged between 21 to 26,
and in the industry of retail.
Chris came to us absolutely exhausted.
He had tried to raise health and safety concerns with his
employer on multiple occasions, both formally and informally,
and all of these attempts to raise concerns were ignored.
Chris had been recently made a supervisor, but he hadn't been given any
training or support for how to look after the people he was
supervising and the training he needed to be safe in that role.
He was also expected to do both his new role and his old role without
having any increase of hours.
There was an understaffing issue kind of rife throughout the workplace, and
he was really noticing in the supervisor role that there was an increase of
minor injuries, largely due to fatigue and burnout and
juggling all of these multiple jobs.
There was also some machinery health and safety concerns that he had that he
raised, and again, these were
completely ignored. And when it finally reached the point that it was then
impacting his mental health, he took it further to his employer, and
his employer's response was to laugh in his face.
These are the words that Chris wrote to us when he
first got in contact with us. He said, "I'm dealing with a
workplace issue. I raised concerns about understaffing and needing more support.
When it finally started affecting my mental health, I then raised the issue more
seriously and formally. I was direct but professional and
respectful, but I feel as though this has been unhelpful and my employer is now
engaging in bad faith. It's really affecting my mental health, but I
also feel strongly that people can't be treated like this, and I'm unsure of
what to do. It's left me feeling really unconfident.
I feel exhausted because all I want was an acknowledgment that the issues I
raised were not nothing. I really want some help with my options."
As you can hear in that, he's just exhausted.
He's tried everything he can to get these health and safety concerns that are
completely valid to be heard. So we supported Chris
in raising a personal grievance that was successful, and the
success of this PG largely relied upon the current act, the Health and
Safety at Work 2015 Act.
It was really integral to help
explain to the employer why he needed to listen to this.
And honestly, the employer's attitude kind of
aligned with a lot of the attitude of the current bill, which was that...
Oh, sorry, of the proposed bill rather, which was that if it wasn't a
critical risk, if it wasn't life or death, then the health and safety risk didn't
matter. However, what Chris was saying was actually that it
should matter because they were having real-life implications
to the workplace.
Through this process, and because of the current act, the 2015 Health and
Safety Act, we were able to have discussions with the employer, and through those
discussions, the employer was able to understand that
the
concerns raised by this young worker were indeed valid
concerns.
That health and safety risks that can seem minor can actually have
really serious harm, and that harm can be
costly and lengthy to recover from.
And that also having expectations and assumptions about a
worker's ability to protect themself in the workplace without then giving them
any support or training, in regards to those health and safety
risks is unfair and it sets the worker up for failure.
This story is really important to share with you today because it
highlights the real-life stories and
struggles that young workers, and I'm sure many other workers, are
already having when it comes to raising health and safety concerns.
And what we're really worried about is that the
proposed bill will further legitimize and
encourage employers to dismiss these concerns that are
entirely valid.
We see that young workers already struggle to raise
these health and safety concerns with their employers, and we believe that
not only
will this bill discourage young workers from
raising these concerns, but if they do raise concerns, they will no
longer have the mechanism or the legislation to fall back on to
back up their claims that these concerns are valid and needed to
be heard.
Similarly, really worried about how that's going to affect their training,
and really worried about how that's going to affect their overall
wellbeing and their lifelong
careers within the New Zealand workforce.
So yeah, kia ora. I'll leave it there if there's any couple of more minutes left
for questions.
Kia ora.
Any questions from the committee?
Phil.
Thanks, Matariki. The story of Chris was very illustrative.
You footnoted it in your submission, but I wondered if you
could flesh out what it is about young workers that
makes them more likely to
experience psychosocial harm in the workplace, because that's, I think,
kind of key to your submission, isn't it?
Thank you. Yeah.
There are a whole heap of contributing factors to why young workers are more
vulnerable to psychosocial harm.
There are a lot of assumptions about young workers.
Young workers are also frequently juggling
multiple responsibilities, whether that's study, as well as taking care of
family, as well as, they tend to be in that part-time
work, which also means they're juggling multiple
psychosocial factors.
Yeah. There's also,
I don't know, there's a really big kind of assumption and
stereotyping that happens with young workers that
also kind of has this compounding effect where young
workers are seen to be snowflakes or they're seen to
be not hard enough for the workplace, and
so
we will often minimize the
sharing of the harm that we are experiencing, and then that
compounds over time.
Yeah.
Great. Thank you very much, Matariki, for your presentation
at the hearing this morning. That's all we've got time for, but I'm sure you'll
continue to follow progress of this bill.
Thank you.
Members, we're now moving to our next submission from
Business
New Zealand, and I think we've got Catherine Rich online for
that. Good morning, Catherine.
Good morning. I'm just wondering how I get the camera
start.
No worries. We'll give you a moment. There we go.
No.
Move it.
We have
10 minutes with you this morning.
So that includes any time for questions. Over to you.
Oh, thank you. Thank you, Chairman.
Members of the committee, thanks for the opportunity to appear today.
May I introduce Catherine Beard. She's Business New Zealand's Director of
Advocacy. Business New Zealand supports this bill.
Business New Zealand is the country's largest business advocacy organization,
representing businesses of all sizes across all
sectors.
In forming our position, we have drawn on nationwide
feedback from small business focus groups, which was reflected in
our report, Reducing Compliance Burden on New Zealand Small
Businesses, as well as MBIE's nationwide consultation through
its Have Your Say on Health and Safety Roadshow.
And we've also recently tested a
response with a number of small business leaders.
Health and safety in New Zealand workplaces is a core part of
our advocacy work. That is why maintaining a
strong and clear duty of care on all employers was fundamental
to our support of this bill. At its heart, this bill is about
making New Zealand's health and safety system work better in practice,
particularly for small business.
And that reflects exactly what we have heard on the ground.
We are clear at the outset, the goal is not less health and safety, it is
smarter, more proportionate health and safety.
Nothing in this bill removes the fundamental duty of
care on any business to provide a safe
workplace. That obligation remains unchanged, as it
should. What this bill does is right-size how that duty
is applied, especially for small, low-risk
businesses. Through our work, we heard consistent feedback.
The system for small businesses is too complex.
There is uncertainty about what good looks like, and there
is a real fear of getting it wrong. That fear has
consequences. Small business owners told us it leads
to over-compliance, a reliance on consultants,
and a focus on paperwork rather than real risk.
One restaurant and hospitality advocate told me that some
small cafes and restaurants can spend thousands of dollars on
consultants, only to be told coffee is hot,
knives are sharp, and to watch out for trips.
That's not improving safety. That's a system that, in some
circumstances, has become over-engineered and disconnected from
common sense. This bill introduces a
clearer focus on critical risks, the risks that can cause
serious harm. It also strengthens practical guidance through
approved codes of practice,
which will bring greater certainty, particularly for
small business. It reduces duplication across regulatory
systems and provides greater clarity around duty
and responsibilities. Business New Zealand supports all of these
changes, because risk is not evenly distributed.
A small PR firm, insurance office, or cafe does
not face the same risks as a construction site or a factory.
Treating them the same adds costs, adds confusion,
and does not improve safety outcomes.
When compliance becomes too complex, small businesses can
disengage or outsource responsibility.
Small businesses have given us feedback about generic
templates, box ticking, and systems that
exist on paper but not in practice.
That is not good health and safety.
Good safety comes from understanding your risks and managing
them, not from navigating complexity.
Small businesses are not asking for lower standards of safety.
They are asking for clarity, simplicity, and confidence
that they are meeting their obligations.
As one
business told us, "We would really benefit if you just tell us
what we need to do."They do not want to rely on a
compliance industry to interpret their obligations.
For low-risk small businesses, it makes sense to focus
on the critical risks.
It is important to emphasize high-risk sectors will continue to face
robust obligations. Critical risks for all
must be identified and managed. Worker
safety remains paramount. This reform is about
aligning effort with actual risk, not reducing
protection.
And to be clear, this is not about trivializing everyday
risks. It is about making sure the system is simple enough to be
applied effectively in real workplaces.
Across the business community, there is broad agreement on the problem,
complexity, and uncertainty. Where views might differ
is how best to solve it.
Business New Zealand's view is that this bill is the right
direction, provided it delivers clarity and
practicality for those applying it day to day.
Chair and members, this bill is a practical response to
what we have heard directly from small businesses across New
Zealand.
This bill does not lower standards, it makes them workable.
If we get this right, small businesses will spend less time on
paperwork and more time doing what matters most, keeping their people
safe and running their businesses.
Small business owners understand their responsibility to keep employees
safe. Asking small, low-risk businesses to focus
on critical risks is proportionate,
practical, and sensible.
Thank you to the committee, and we welcome your comments and questions.
Thanks very much, Catherine. First question from Grant McCallum.
Yeah, good morning, Catherine. One of the issues that's come
up is, and you've mentioned it here in your submission, is where you've
got a small business operating a large business site, right, and
they're operating at potentially different levels of health and safety,
what is your suggestion then to address that?
You talk about here extra guidance may be needed.
What are you thinking of in that space?
Well, when larger businesses have a
large workplace, such as a construction site or a
factory,
they have very high standards, very high requirements, and
you agree to uphold those requirements and
fulfill health and safety responsibilities as per the
requirements of those businesses, i.e.,
the customers of the small business.
So we haven't
really seen any evidence that that would change,
because say, for example, an architect is visiting a large
construction site. They will turn up to that site, they will put their hard hat
on, they will put their boots on, they will sign in, they will often
have a safety induction. So the safety in those premises remains
the same. So, Kathryn, you might like to add.
Yeah. I think it's just we're not suggesting any
legislative tweaks. We just think it's a communication issue.
I think we have heard through
some of the feedback that there may be some confusion as to
whether the bigger company's responsible for the architect's
office, I guess. They're responsible in a low-risk
environment. If you think about it, an architect is sort of running
a paper-based business or is on computers and things.
The question is, is the big
site that has more risks, are they then responsible, do
they have any responsibility for that architect when they're working back in their
office? We would say no, but there's a bit of
confusion around that, so we just think it's a communication and a clarity
issue, and that could probably be done on websites and
things.
Thank you. Mike-
No, go on.
Sorry, Grant. Quick-
No, no. Go on
... through your time.
Go on.
Yeah. Mike Davidson, your question.
Sure. Thank you, Catherine, for your submission.
Just
wondering if your view on mental health and workplace
management is a critical risk.
Certainly, it is something that all employers are
aware of.
Look, it could be
if mental health does develop in a workplace, these are issues of
management and good leadership.
Certainly, within a workplace, these are things that employers do consider,
and they will work extremely hard to provide safe
workplaces for all aspects of safety.
In terms of managing the critical risks, though, for small businesses such
as cafes,
small retail shops, et cetera,
these are issues that good managers are very aware of, so
issues of
mental health, stress, et cetera.
Look, thank you, Catherine. I think that's all we've got time for this morning.
Bill, I'll give you the first question in the next round.
If we can
move to Gregory
Lowney,
and we'll see you again, I'm sure, Catherine. Thank you.
While we're waiting for Gregory to come up, just to confirm for the
committee's minutes,
that from 11:00 a.m., by agreement of the
committee, Phil Twyford will chair the committee through the end of
the meeting.
Morning, Gregory.
We have five minutes with you this morning, including any time for questions.
I'll hand over to you
Thank you. Good morning,
and thank you, Mr. Chair and members of the committee.
My name is Gregory Ioane, and I'm speaking from a frontline
security perspective.
I support this
bill in principle. Moving toward clearer rules, less
unnecessary compliance,
and a stronger focus on serious harm is the
right direction.
But my main message is simple.
For the security sector, we must make sure
that focusing on critical risk does not
overlook the most common risk
we face, which is violence.
In this job, violence is not rare.
It's not theoretical. It's something security deal with regularly.
Security officers work every day in
supermarkets, hospitals, courts,
bars, and public spaces.
Security officers deal with theft, aggression,
intoxication, and mental health situations.
Often, security
are the first to step in when something goes wrong.
So when we talk about critical risk in this sector,
we are talking about assaults,
weapons, group violence, and workers being
left alone without support.
The idea of critical risk in this bill
is a good step,
but it only works if it is clearly understood
and applied. If there is any uncertainty,
if violence is not treated as a top risk,
then the reform risks missing what really
matters
for this workforce.
My first point is this:
violence against frontline security workers should be clearly
recognized as a critical risk.
My second point is about small businesses.
The bill reduces some requirements for smaller operators,
which can make
sense, but in this industry,
that can create problems and risks.
A lot of security work is
done by smaller companies and subcontractors.
There is a real risk that violence can become seen
as just part of the job and not properly managed.
If a guard is sent alone into a high-risk situation without good
communication, backup, or proper equipment,
that is not efficiency, it is putting them at risk of
serious harm.
So the principle must be clear.
If there is a serious risk,
it is a critical
risk no matter the size of the business.
My third point is about what good safety practice looks
like.
We already know the risks and we already know
what works to manage them. Avoiding lone
workers in high-risk situations, reliable
communication and duress systems, proper protective equipment,
clear de-escalation procedures, and good training and
supervision.
These are not-
Gregory, can I just--
Do you mind if I interrupt and just say we've read the
submission. Is there anything you want to add to the submission before I give an
opportunity for one or two questions? Otherwise, you'll run out of time.
I think we can run straight to the questions if you want the questions.
It's pretty much the same.
Yeah, it might just be a more valuable way of, yeah, of getting some-
Indeed
... insight for the team.
Phil, did you have a question on this one?
Sure. Thanks, Chair. Gregory, thanks for the submission.
I think the issue about the risk of violence for people in the
security
industry is extremely important.
What's your reading of the bill
as it stands, and whether or not that risk of violence is
brought under the umbrella of critical risk?
Sorry, Honorable Phil Twyford. Can you re-ask me the question
again?
Yeah. Is it your understanding that the risk of violence
faced by people in the security industry is covered under the
definition of critical risk in the bill?
I think that it is covered,
but it's only covered if it's managed properly.
Yeah. Okay.
And we've got the resources, and we have the resources.
Currently,
Phil, we haven't got the resources.
Mm.
It's the resources. We're undertrained.
Yep.
This industry's outdated. I mean, we just look at the consist-- I mean,
just last year we had a fatality in this industry.
Mm.
I mean,
in the same year, we had another female staff-- I mean, it's just at
Countdown. I mean, they're just walking out of the store with these goods, and
it's crime on one hand, and then we're
trained to intervene and just do the right thing.
I mean, they just turn around and assault us.
I mean, it's just-
Mm. Yeah.
Can I ask a follow-up, Chair?
Yeah. Thanks, Phil. Yeah. We'll give you a quick follow-up. Yeah.
So, as you've noticed, as you've said in your submission, Gregory, the
bill basically exempts small businesses under 20
employees from having to be responsible for managing the health and safety
risks associated with the sort of psychosocial.
And yet we know for a lot of security people, stress
caused by the
work is a major factor for people.
Do you not think that the majority of security industries who
are small SMEs, small businesses under 20 employees,
do you not think they should be covered by those psychosocial risks?
No, I think they should be covered by those psychosocial risks.
You do?
I do.
Yeah. Okay.
Look, Gregory, thank you very much for your submission this morning.
Okay.
You've made some really important points for us, and
your submission and the way you tied it to the values of a particular
party to make us think about the questions was, I thought, very clever.
So well done.
Thank you.
Thanks very much for your submission this morning. No worries.
We're now going to invite Horticulture New Zealand for 10
minutes, including time for questions.
We've got Sarah Cameron and Kate. Morning, Sarah.
Hi. Good morning, everybody, and thank you for the opportunity presented
today. My name is Sarah Cameron, and I lead the policy
work on health and safety matters at HortNZ.
With me is Kate Truffitt, who is the chief executive of
Potatoes New Zealand. Kate has previously chaired the Kiwi Fruit Industry
Health and Safety Committee and the HortNZ Health and Safety
Council, and I have sat on be- both of those with her.
We have taken our submission as read and are generally supportive of the
proposed changes. However, one of our key points is that we
consider greater clarity is needed on how the definition of
a small PCBU applies where multiple
PCBU's operate at the same workplace, such as where
growers engage contractors or labor hire companies.
I'm just going to hand over to Kate now, who will quickly talk more
about this, and then we can move on to questions. Thank you.
Kate.
Good morning. I just wanted to talk you through a couple of examples
to make this more real. So farms and orchards can have a lot of
different workers on them, and these workers can have lots of different
duties, so mixed duties, but they also can be employed by
different PCBUs. So we're concerned
that there's not enough maybe clarity within the
legislation on the application of a small
PCBU and how these can work together.
We're leaning a lot on codes of practices here,
and we have a live example at the moment where we have been
working with WorkSafe on a code of practice.
But they have chosen to actually make horticulture out of
the scope of this particular codes of practice.
This is roles and responsibility which this would fall squarely on,
which seems quite ridiculous. I represent potato growers,
we are mixed farming and quite often are grown with
arable or
with farm animals.
So I can't see how you could apply a code of practice
to one part of your business, but not the other.
So I think when it comes to developing these guidance, which this
legislation will lean on, it's going to be incredibly important to make
sure that all of industry is covered and not just
pick and choose which piece of industry is covered.
Thank you.
Handing back to Sarah.
Thanks. Thanks very much.
All right.
Sarah, yeah?
Yep, just invite any questions. Thanks.
Great. Thank you. Questions from members.
I'll kick off with two questions. One is, in your submission, you talk
about the calculation of when you're under and over 20 people.
One of the understandings I have of my reading is

So firstly, I was optimistic and I was hopeful that this bill would shift
and move the dial for health and safety performance in New Zealand.
However, after reviewing this bill,
I have to say I was quite underwhelmed, unimpressed, and
a little bit disappointed to be honest with you.
So I asked myself two questions when I was reading this, "Does
this bill
improve the overall performance of health and safety in Aotearoa, New Zealand?"
I'll say no.
"Does this bill help me as a health and safety rep
to improve the overall performance
of health and safety?" Again, I'll probably have to say no.
So for me, from a big picture perspective, this bill doesn't
represent the worker voice in any meaningful way.
And to me, that is a fundamental flaw within this document,
and I believe it is a missed opportunity.
So I couldn't believe it. I looked through it again, and I
control, find, search,
and tried to look for WEPA, Worker Engagement, Participation and
Representation. The word "worker" comes up 23 times.
"Engagement" comes up
zero times. "Participation" zero times.
"Representation" only appears once, and "representative" turns
up
twice in this whole document.
So to me, as a worker,
that speaks quite highly, and it's not just the numbers, it
actually highlights that there's a clear
measurable gap between legislation and the
workers.
Worker engagement, participation, and representation, they're not optional
extras. They are the backbone
to a well-functioning health and safety system.
And right now, that backbone is missing from this document,
and quite frankly, it's not good enough.
So
this new learning could be quite constructive.
Don't take it as a criticism, but more of an opportunity.
I'd rather you take it as an opportunity to backfill the worker voice
within this legislation, an opportunity to move forward
and beyond compliance on paper and improve the compliance in practice, an
opportunity to enable workers and their representatives, not just
expect them to carry out responsibility without influence.
Because where I stand,
workers don't need more theory.
They just need practical ways to be heard, involved, and effective in the systems
that are meant to keep them safe.
So I acknowledge that this bill reflects feedback from small businesses,
industries,
business leaders, landowners, et cetera,
and the concerns around, that they have with compliance burden,
uncertainty, and fear.
But I must ask you guys,
where's the worker voice in this conversation? What about their concerns?
What about their burdens? What about their fear, their uncertainty?
So in closing, if we're serious about improving the overall health and safety
outcomes of Aotearoa, then workers and representatives must be
visible, must be heard,
and WEPA must be embedded in legislation, not absent from it.
So, Mr. Chair, just a shout-out to you guys.
If you need any follow-up questions or any assistance
on this after,
I'm only happy to help because it looks like you guys need it.
So just, I'll pass it back to you guys. Kia ora.
Excellent. Thanks,
Mel. I think we've run out of time for questions, but your
point's very well made, so thank you.
No worries. Thank you.
Appreciate it.
All right. We've got Andrew Hayden as our personal
submitter, last one of the day.
Hello, Andrew. Welcome to the subcommittee.
You want to turn on your camera and join us?
Camera.
Can you see me?
No, not yet.
I've got a new computer, and it's
probably going to flummox me.
I can't see any...
That's all right, Andrew. Don't-
Oh, I've got it.
You have. Okay. All right. You're all good.
So welcome to the committee. We've got your submission and have read it, and
you've got five minutes, including questions.
Thank you. Having listened to a number of the other submissions
you've already heard, I've had to actually tick off a number of the points that I
would already want to make. So if this doesn't last very long, it's because
you've already heard a lot of the things that I would have to say.
But I've been in the health and safety domain for 48 years,
which is a hell of a long time in this very, very difficult domain.
But if we're looking at the problem of complexity,
it's only complex when you look at the whole domain, and
I don't blame anybody for not being able to get their head around what goes on in
the health and safety domain, especially for professionals.
But when you walk through the door of any sort of
PCBU, a small one or a medium-sized one, you're not dealing
with the complexity of the whole domain itself.
You're dealing with the complexities of that particular
business.
And if that business owner, who's probably built the business in many
cases themselves, they will be intimately aware of the
things that can knock their business sideways and potentially put them out of
business.
So the knowledge of exactly what kinds of
risks the PCBU's facing are probably well
known to the PCBU and/or the
employees themselves, who are the workers who do the work, who get exposed to
the risks.
So I think complexity is a furphy.
It seems complex when you stand back far enough and look at the entire domain and
what it applies to, everything from ergonomics to chemistry, to all sorts of
things. But a PCBU only has to get a fix on exactly what
is it that my operations are doing to create risks of
harm to the people that are exposed to my work, and that's not
an exhaustive list at all.
My son is actually a small panel beater.
He's got six employees and an apprentice.
And when I sat down with him the other day to help him get his head around this
whole thing,
I said, "Just manage risk."
So in 48 years of looking at every kind of
business you could imagine, from international corporations to tiny little
businesses in Dannyburg,
you can actually whittle it down to those three words, just manage
risk.
And if the PCBU, the owner of the business,
understood, I'm going to read Section 44 (4) , which
to me is the most critical piece of the entire legislation, "To gain an
understanding of the nature of the operations of the business or undertaking
of the PCBU and generally of the hazards and risks associated with
those operations." Not the whole domain.
They don't have to learn everything.
But if they sat with their workers for five minutes and said, "What could cause us
harm here, boys, or people?"
They would immediately have access to the things that go on in their
operation, and then what do we do to control it?
It really is that simple. It's shocking to
say that after 48 years, that's all I've learned.
But just manage risk. And it's not the risk of everything, it's the risk
of occupational harm to the people who we employ and the work that's being
done out in the field. There's another, quickly,
there's a thing that I don't know if I coined the phrase, safety in the
flow of work.
Workers are exposed to the risk of harm, and people
exposed to that work itself in the public domain.
But that's where risk needs to be addressed.
A critical risk can emerge out of a change in the weather or
a different team member joining the team halfway through a job.
It's in the flow of work, and this is something that the last speaker
pointed out. The workers need to be able to put their hand up and say, "Hang on a
second,
Mr. Boss.
Do you understand what's happening right here, right now that could cause us harm?"
And if they don't get access to be able to put their hand up and say, well, they do
have access under the law to say, "Hang on a second.
This needs to stop for a minute while we sort this mess out, otherwise
somebody will get harmed." The PCBU needs to listen and give them the
right to actually express their concerns about how to control the risk of harm
in that particular circumstance.
Critical risks are not immutable.
Critical risks can be infinitely well managed and not be a
critical risk because of the level of management going on.
But a small job can turn into a critical risk if somebody does something
foolish or inadvertent in a moment.
So that definition of critical risk is really
it's up to the PCBU to actually sit down with his
people and ask the question, "What could harm
us?" And then to go into the next stage, which is, are the
critical risks that we've identified together, are they being
effectively managed? That's the performance-based issue that
Robins came up with. Doesn't have to be consulted with
every consultant on the planet. It's just, what should we do
to control the risk of harm in our circumstances?
And I'd like the regulator to be able to say to every
PCBU out there, "Have you identified the risks of harm
to your people? And if not, it's a prima facie breach of Section
So let's get on with it. Let's sit down with everybody, come up with a simple
risk assessment process, and make sure everybody's doing it
reasonably diligently. And if not, I'm sorry,
we're going to kick you.
Andrew, thank you.
Clear submission. We've run out of time-
... but you've made a useful submission on things that other people have not talked
about, so it's helpful. Thank you.
Thank you.
Great.
Okay. Hey, so colleagues,
that's it, and the clerk has informed me that
we will have time for consideration at the next full meeting of the
committee when we've got everybody there, including both
subcommittees. So
we're done.
Taryn, is there anything else
you want us to know about or discuss?
No, that's everything from me.
Cool. Thanks for all your support.
That's it. We're doing the private session, no private session. All that's good.
Perfect.
No
